title: Cisco HyperFlex Cluster Health
exclusions: The stub sourcetype `hci:cluster_health` is a fictional name that no Splunk TA or Cisco feed emits. HCI is a category, not a vendor namespace, so a single string like that cannot be used as a `sourcetype=`. The real, implementable path is a customer-owned HEC or scripted poller to HX Connect writing `cisco:hyperflex:cluster`, `cisco:hyperflex:health`, `cisco:hyperflex:node`, and `cisco:hyperflex:datastore` (plus `cisco:hyperflex:alarm` when you index alarms) as this UC documents, or the Cisco Intersight `cisco:intersight:hyperflex*` family from Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight on [Splunkbase 7828](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7828), with Cisco UCS TA 2731 as the hardware back-stop. This UC is not a substitute for per-VM or per-datastore VMware performance counters, for Cisco SD-WAN or application-layer synthetic monitoring, or for a future dedicated SAN use case, and it does not read Prism or Nutanix APIs or VxRail JSON—those are UC-19.2.25 and UC-19.2.13. It also does not stand in for a Windows-only Storage Spaces story (UC-19.2.21) or a VMware vSAN disk group UC (19.2.17). The corrected sourcetype trail is `hci:cluster_health` (wrong) to `cisco:hyperflex:*` from a customer script plus optional `cisco:intersight:hyperflex*` and filtered `cisco:ucs:*` for chassis-level faults the cluster layer might not bubble.
evidence: Saved search `ss_uc_19_2_1_hyperflex_cluster_health` on `index=hyperflex` (or the customer index) with 13+ month online retention, version-controlled `lookups/hyperflex_cluster_owner.csv` with peer-reviewed edits, and an optional PDF export to `idx_evidence` per Sev-1 after you attach the `table` from this UC plus a matching Cisco Intersight or HX UI screenshot in the runbook page.
dataSources: `index=hyperflex` (rename if your `inputs.conf` uses `index=hci` or a department-specific name) for customer-defined sourcetypes: **`cisco:hyperflex:cluster`** from `GET /rest/clusters` (cluster `name`/`uuid`/`state`/`dataReplicationFactor` or `replicationFactor` plus **access** policy and accessibility when present); **`cisco:hyperflex:health`** from `GET /rest/health` for **HX**-wide `healthState`/`healthLevel` and resiliency text; **`cisco:hyperflex:node`** from `GET /rest/nodes` for `role` in {`STORAGE`,`COMPUTE`}, per-node `status`/`version`; **`cisco:hyperflex:datastore`** from `GET /rest/datastores` for `mountSummary`/`accessibility` strings; **`cisco:hyperflex:alarm`** (optional) from `GET /rest/alarms` or the shipped alias for the active **messages** list. **Authentication** is a short-lived `accessToken` (often ~30 minutes) from `POST /rest/aaa/login` with **Basic** credentials, used as `Authorization: Bearer <token>` until refresh. Secondary path: **Cisco Intersight** [Splunkbase 7828](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7828) with `cisco:intersight:hyperflexcluster` from the `hyperflex.Clusters` managed object, `cisco:intersight:hyperflexnode` from `hyperflex.Node`, and `cisco:intersight:hyperflexalarm` from `hyperflex.Alarm`. Tertiary: **Cisco UCS** [2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731) for `cisco:ucs:faults` and `cisco:ucs:inventory` filtered to the same physical estate.
app: You install no Splunkbase “HyperFlex TA.” Instead, ship a local app (for example `TA_hx_collector`) that contains the scripted input, HEC tokens, and `props` stanzas for the `cisco:hyperflex:*` names, plus a wrapper `lookups/hyperflex_cluster_owner.csv` and `transforms.conf` stanza `hyperflex_cluster_owner`. For Intersight coverage, add Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight from [7828](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7828) on the collection tier and on Search Heads. For Cisco UCS hardware, keep Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs from [2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731) with the same inventory filters you use in UC-19.1.4.
spl:
`comment("UC-19.2.1 — Cisco HyperFlex cluster health: HEC from HX Connect REST; sourcetypes cisco:hyperflex:cluster, cisco:hyperflex:health, cisco:hyperflex:node, cisco:hyperflex:datastore; coalesce; hyperflex_cluster_owner; RF, datastore, and freeze gating. Replace index= if your site differs.")`
index=hyperflex (sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:health" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:node" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:datastore") earliest=-2h@h
| eval cluster_uuid=toString(coalesce(cluster_uuid, clusterUuid, cluster_id, clusterId, uuid, id, ""))
| eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name, displayName, ""))
| eval acc_sum=toString(coalesce(accessibility_summary, accessibilitySummary, mountSummary, accessPolicy, mount_state, dataAccessibility, ""))
| eval stat_cluster=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster", toString(coalesce(healthState, health_state, state, clusterState, "")), null())
| eval stat_health=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:health", toString(coalesce(healthState, health_state, resiliencyState, resiliency_state, overallHealth, "")), null())
| eval res_txt=toString(coalesce(resiliencyDetails, resiliencyState, resiliency_state, ""))
| eval hs_line=toString(coalesce(stat_health, stat_cluster, healthState, health_state, ""))
| eval hpart=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:health" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster", tonumber(coalesce(healthLevel, health_level, severity, "0")), null())
| fillnull value=0 hpart
| eval reb=if(match(lower(hs_line." ".res_txt),"(?i)rebuildinprogress|rebuild\\s*in\\s*progress|rebuild"),1,0)
| eval rf=tonumber(coalesce(replication_factor, replicationFactor, dataReplicationFactor, data_replication_factor, "0"))
| eval c_acc_risk=if((sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:datastore") AND (len(acc_sum) > 0) AND (NOT (acc_sum IN ("FullyAccessible", "Online"))), 1, 0)
| eval d_ds=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:datastore" AND (len(acc_sum) > 0) AND (match(lower(acc_sum),"(?i)unmount|inaccess|unhealthy|degraded|offline|failed|error") OR (NOT (acc_sum IN ("FullyAccessible", "Online", "Healthy", "OK", "Mounted", "Up")))), 1, 0)
| eval nstat=toString(coalesce(node_status, nodeStatus, status, opState, state, ""))
| eval node_nm=toString(coalesce(node_name, nodeName, host_name, hostName, hostname, name, ""))
| eval nver=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:node" AND (len(node_nm) > 0), toString(coalesce(hxVersion, hxdp_version, version, softwareVersion, "")), null())
| eval nbad=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:node", if(match(lower(nstat),"(?i)up|online|healthy|ok|normal|active|connected|ready"),0, if(match(lower(nstat),"(?i)down|unreach|failed|inact|unhealthy|unres|absent|poweroff|offline|error"),1,0)),0)
| where (len(cluster_uuid) > 0) OR (len(cluster_name) > 0)
| fillnull value="(unnamed)" cluster_name
| eval c_uuid=if(len(cluster_uuid) > 0, cluster_uuid, "name:".cluster_name)
| eval c_name=cluster_name
| stats max(_time) AS last_poll max(rf) AS cluster_rf max(hpart) AS hlv_m max(reb) AS reb_fl max(c_acc_risk) AS acc_risk max(d_ds) AS ds_unmount sum(nbad) AS node_risk_count values(eval(if(nbad=1, node_nm, null()))) AS affected_nodes max(eval(if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:node", _time, null()))) AS last_node_ev dc(nver) AS ver_dc latest(hs_line) AS health_state BY c_uuid, c_name
| eval cluster_uuid=c_uuid
| eval cluster_name=c_name
| eval mixed_ver=if(ver_dc > 1, 1, 0)
| eval stale=if((now() - last_poll) > 900, 1, 0)
| lookup local=true hyperflex_cluster_owner cluster_uuid AS c_uuid OUTPUT business_service, owner_email, expected_replication_factor, eol_phase, change_freeze_until
| fillnull value="" expected_replication_factor
| eval exp_rf=if((isnotnull(tonumber(expected_replication_factor))) AND (tonumber(expected_replication_factor) > 0), tonumber(expected_replication_factor), 2)
| eval rf_breach=if((isnotnull(cluster_rf)) AND (cluster_rf > 0) AND (cluster_rf < exp_rf), 1, 0)
| eval cft=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
| eval cft2=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%d")
| eval freeze_ts=coalesce(cft, cft2)
| eval freeze_active=if((isnotnull(freeze_ts)) AND (now() < freeze_ts), 1, 0)
| eval hs=lower(health_state)
| eval cpath=if(match(hs, "(?i)unhealthy|critical|rfnotmet"), 1, 0)
| eval is_crit=if((cpath=1) OR (rf_breach=1) OR (ds_unmount=1) OR (acc_risk=1) OR (node_risk_count >= 2), 1, 0)
| eval hdeg=if(match(hs, "(?i)degraded"), 1, 0)
| eval hwar=if(match(hs, "(?i)warning"), 1, 0)
| eval is_high=if((is_crit=0) AND (hdeg=1 OR hwar=1 OR (node_risk_count=1) OR ((reb_fl=1) AND (hlv_m >= 2))), 1, 0)
| eval is_medium=if((is_crit=0) AND (is_high=0) AND ((reb_fl=1 AND hlv_m=1) OR (match(hs, "(?i)attention|pending|rebalanc|resync|repair"))), 1, 0)
| eval is_low=if((is_crit=0) AND (is_high=0) AND (is_medium=0) AND ((stale=1) OR (mixed_ver=1)), 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=case((is_crit=1), "critical", (is_high=1), "high", (is_medium=1), "medium", (is_low=1), "low", 1=1, "ok")
| eval severity_bucket=if((freeze_active=1) AND (severity_bucket!="critical"), "ok", severity_bucket)
| eval sev_order=case((severity_bucket="critical"),1,(severity_bucket="high"),2,(severity_bucket="medium"),3,(severity_bucket="low"),4,(severity_bucket="ok"),5,1=1,6)
| table last_poll, cluster_name, c_uuid, health_state, cluster_rf, exp_rf, rf_breach, acc_risk, ds_unmount, node_risk_count, affected_nodes, hlv_m, reb_fl, stale, mixed_ver, business_service, owner_email, eol_phase, change_freeze_until, freeze_active, severity_bucket, sev_order
| sort 0 sev_order, cluster_name
description: Aggregates the customer-owned cisco:hyperflex:* HEC feed that polls HX Connect GET /rest/health together with cisco:hyperflex:cluster inventory for dataReplicationFactor (or replicationFactor on some HX builds) and with cisco:hyperflex:node plus cisco:hyperflex:datastore to catch resiliency state drift, RF below the configured policy, datastore rows that are not FullyAccessible or Online, and two or more down STORAGE or COMPUTE members in the lookback. The coalesce() set bridges camel and snake case because HX JSON differs between 4.0, 4.5, and 5.0; the hyperflex_cluster_owner lookup enforces expected_replication_factor, and freeze_active handles change_freeze_until with both ISO-8601 and date-only forms. This is not UC-19.2.25 (Nutanix Prism and Splunk_TA_nutanix CVM service maps), not UC-19.2.13 (Dell VxRail vxm HEC, different management plane), not UC-19.2.21 (Azure Stack HCI PowerShell and S2D), not UC-19.2.7 (Nutanix CVM Stargate or Cassandra, not cisco:hyperflex:node), and not UC-19.2.17 (vSAN disk groups) because the storage control plane is HX Data Platform, not vSAN. Pair with UC-19.1.4 for Cisco UCS FRU-level faults, because a green cluster row can co-exist with red UCS power or disk inventory.
value: A Cisco HyperFlex cluster is still the resiliency boundary that keeps retail, healthcare imaging, and factory floor virtual machines on ESXi from sharing fate with a single Cisco blade failure. When the RF the API reports falls below the design value your governance row expects, or the datastore list stops showing Online, you are one step away from an incident class that is closer to a regional ESXi brown-out or VMDK timeout storm than a single noisy host metric. The business cares because the alert names the `business_service` and `eol_phase` of an estate that is no longer on the new-order price list but can remain under Cisco support to 2034-09-30 per the [HyperFlex EOL](https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/products/collateral/hyperconverged-infrastructure/hyperflex-hx-series/hyperflex-eol.html) fact sheet, so you can hand audit and finance teams a dated Splunk result row, not a screenshot from a controller that a fired engineer can no longer log into. The outcome is a smaller bridge with fewer wrong guesses when vCenter looks fine at the VM layer but HyperFlex is rebuilding after an NVMe loss you already see on UCS inventory.
implementation: Create `index=hyperflex` and HEC. Place `TA_hx_collector` on a Heavy Forwarder in the HX management zone with a scripted input and `props.conf` stanzas for each `cisco:hyperflex:*` sourcetype. Add `lookups/hyperflex_cluster_owner.csv` with a `transforms.conf` `hyperflex_cluster_owner` entry keyed on `cluster_uuid`. Run the `spl` interactively, then schedule it every 5 minutes, throttle on `c_name` plus `severity_bucket`, and route `critical` to PagerDuty using the `sev_order` column so `freeze`-demoted rows never wake the NOC. Wire optional Intersight 7828 and UCS 2731 in parallel for fleet and FRU context.
visualization: Row 1 – single value counts for `critical` and `high` `severity` rows plus a timechart of HEC event volume for each `cisco:hyperflex:*` sourcetype. Row 2 – the final `table` with colour on `severity_bucket` and a drill to raw JSON for a chosen cluster key. Row 3 – optional `xyseries` heat for datastore `mountSummary` per cluster if you have fewer than twenty clusters, otherwise a child search. Row 4 – a link-out panel to the Cisco Intersight cluster page when the Intersight path is in use, and a second link to the UCS hardware dashboard from UC-19.1.4 for the same site code.

references.title: Cisco — HyperFlex end-of-new-sale and software support (EOL)
Cisco DevNet — HyperFlex / HX Data Platform context

knownFalsePositives: Planned `RebuildInProgress` text while you expand with a canary HX node is a normal, hours-long resiliency event and should be paired with a change record before you re-open the RF math. A rolling HX service upgrade that walks nodes one at a time can show mixed `version` on `cisco:hyperflex:node` for the thirty-minute window each node is offline, so `ver_dc>1` may trip `mixed_ver` in the low tier even when ESXi is healthy. HyperFlex Edge designs with only two or three nodes can briefly show `RFNotMet` in maintenance because math is different at small N; treat that as a capacity-class finding unless `node_risk_count` stays up. A datastore that reads `unmounted` while an ESXi host is entering maintenance is expected. Controller VM or HX service VM IP work during a planned vSphere PSO can drop polls so `stale=1` fires, compare `_indextime` to `_time` and the poller log. A cluster that says healthy in HyperFlex while `cisco:ucs:faults` still shows PSU or DIMM red is the split between the UCS axis (UC-19.1.4) and this UC, never delete the UCS alert because `HX` is green. Synthetic HEC load tests that replay the same event twice in one second can inflate `dc` counts in a lab; tag those indexes. HyperFlex HX 5.0 can rename string tokens inside `resiliencyDetails`; re-run `fieldsummary` after upgrades before you widen `match` patterns.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites

This is the first cat-19.2 use case in this catalogue to treat Cisco HyperFlex (HX) as a verified, first-class data contract, and it is the bar every future HyperFlex UC in cat-19.2 must extend. The technical detection fuses a customer-owned HEC or scripted poller to HX Connect (`/rest/clusters`, `/rest/health`, `/rest/nodes`, `/rest/datastores`, optional alarm endpoints) with governance columns from a CSV keyed on the cluster UUID your CMDB and backup teams both recognise. There is no Splunkbase “HyperFlex TA” in the 2024–2026 window that ships a turnkey sourcetype list; the implementable world is: (1) custom HEC to HX Connect as the primary SPL in this file expects; (2) Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight on [Splunkbase 7828](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7828) when the tenant already aggregates Intersight-claimed clusters and you want the `cisco:intersight:hyperflexcluster`, `cisco:intersight:hyperflexnode`, and `cisco:intersight:hyperflexalarm` MO families as secondary context; and (3) Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs on [2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731) for UCS bottom-of-stack cisco:ucs:faults and `cisco:ucs:inventory` `storageLocalDisk` rows as a tertiary back-stop, because a quiet cluster string can still sit above PSU or DIMM red when FRU replacement is the real risk.

Cisco’s HyperFlex sales tail ended with a 2024 last-order date while the published software support end date of 2034-09-30 means many enterprises will run mixed-age ESXi clusters on the same HX fabric for a decade after net-new sales stop. Your monitoring program must not assume “end of sale” equals “out of our estate”; instead the `eol_phase` and `change_freeze_until` columns in `hyperflex_cluster_owner.csv` are how the service owner tells Splunk that extended_support still requires the same or higher paging discipline than active, but CAB wants non-critical work quiet during a finance close or ERP go-live. HX Data Platform 4.0, 4.5, and 5.0 can rename JSON fields inside the same REST path, which is the entire reason the `spl` property chains `coalesce(healthState, health_state, state, clusterState)` and similar, not a single `FIELDALIAS-` in `props` alone.

Compare explicitly when you file evidence: this UC is not UC-19.2.25 (Nutanix Prism, Nutanix RF math, and Splunk_TA_nutanix); it is not UC-19.2.13 (Dell VxRail Vxm JSON even though the custom HEC pattern is spiritually similar); it is not UC-19.2.21 (Azure Stack HCI PowerShell and S2D); it is not UC-19.2.7 (CVM Stargate/Cassandra, Nutanix `nutanix:cluster:services_status`); it is not UC-19.2.17 (vSAN disk group objects); and it is the application-layer pair to UC-19.1.4 (UCS FI-level faults), because a healthy cluster readout in HyperFlex does not refute a UCS fault that has not yet bubbled. This UC is the only HyperFlex-verified file in the repository at the time of writing and sets the pattern for all future HX siblings.

`hyperflex_cluster_owner.csv` must ship beside a `default/transforms.conf` stanza `hyperflex_cluster_owner` (filename match, `min_matches=0`, `case_sensitive_match=false` if your UUIDs come mixed-case) with the columns: `cluster_name`, `cluster_uuid`, `business_service`, `owner_email`, `expected_replication_factor` (2 or 3, drives the RF breach path), `eol_phase` (`active`, `extended_support`, `end_of_extended` to route ops triage during the EOL tail), and `change_freeze_until` in ISO-8601 `2026-04-15T18:00:00` or date-only `2026-04-15` for CAB freezes that demote but never erase critical in the SPL below.

The collection host must reach the HX management path on 443, store credentials in a Secrets Manager or `splunksecrets`-backed `passwords.conf` pattern your security team approves, and use an OS-level `cafile=` trust bundle for the cluster certificate rather than a blanket TLS disable, because audit teams treat “verify=False” the same as an unsigned control.


Step 1 — Configure data collection

Build a thin app `TA_hx_collector` under `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/` on the Heavy Forwarder or other node that can open TLS to `https://<hx-mgmt-fqdn-or-ip>/rest/`. The contract is: one poll cycle performs `POST /rest/aaa/login` with Basic credentials, captures `accessToken` (or the field name your build returns), and then reuses `Authorization: Bearer <token>` for `GET /rest/clusters`, `GET /rest/health`, `GET /rest/nodes`, `GET /rest/datastores`, and (optional) `GET /rest/alarms` or the documented `messages` alias, refreshing the token on 401 or a timer near thirty minutes. Emit one JSON line per GET to stdout when you use `script://` or the same object to a HEC URL when you batch—either path is valid as long as `props.conf` sets JSON extraction and a distinct sourcetype for each path so the `spl` field’s `OR` filter matches events.

A production-grade `local/inputs.conf` stanza:

```ini
[script://$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA_hx_collector/bin/hxconnect_poll.sh]
disabled = 0
interval = 90
sourcetype = cisco:hyperflex:cluster
index = hyperflex
```

In practice, use one shell wrapper that calls a single `python3` binary with flags `--out hec` and `--include nodes,datastores,health,alarms` so the interval value matches your agreed 60–120 second cadence; the example is intentionally minimal so Deployment Server diffs are readable. When you do use HEC, create a `hec_token` in Settings → Data → HTTP Event Collector and restrict the token to `index=hyperflex` and source `hxconnect:poll` to simplify RBAC.

`local/props.conf` (Indexer or Heavy) should repeat this pattern for `cisco:hyperflex:cluster`, `cisco:hyperflex:health`, `cisco:hyperflex:node`, `cisco:hyperflex:datastore`, and `cisco:hyperflex:alarm`:

```ini
[cisco:hyperflex:health]
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
LINE_BREAKER = ([\r\n]+)
TRUNCATE = 0
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 32
TIME_PREFIX = ^{\"time\"|\"_time\"
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
KV_MODE = json
```

Map epoch or ISO time fields in the poller so `_time` is the poll instant, not index time only, because the stale branch in the alert assumes you can compare `now()-last_poll` in seconds.

`transforms.conf` in the same wrapper app the peers use for ownership:

```ini
[hyperflex_cluster_owner]
filename = hyperflex_cluster_owner.csv
min_matches = 0
case_sensitive_match = false
```

`metadata/default.meta` should export the transform to any role that runs the saved search, not `admin` alone. Version-control the CSV; treat every new HX cluster like a new firewall line.

TLS pinning: install the Cisco HX controller’s PEM (export from a trusted openssl or browser Save chain flow) into `/opt/splunk/hx-ca/hxconnect.pem` (path is an example) and pass it to Python with `ssl.create_default_context().load_verify_locations('/opt/splunk/hx-ca/hxconnect.pem')` or a single `cafile=` argument. Document that `ssl._create_unverified_context()` is forbidden in your security baseline even when the lab had a self-signed HX cert.

Intersight (Step 1 secondary): after Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight is on the Heavy, enable the HyperFlex MO inputs that hit `/api/v1/hyperflex/Clusters|Nodes|Alarms` so the SPL in Step 2 can be cloned with a second macro that points at `cisco:intersight:hyperflexcluster` when a cluster is not reachable on the local HX IP. That path is the right one for a fleet in one tenant.

UCS (Step 1 tertiary): point Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs at the same Rack / UCSM the HyperFlex nodes register under, then filter `cisco:ucs:faults` with the Service Profile or DN of the ESXi hosts in the HX cluster, and join `cisco:ucs:inventory` for `classId=storageLocalDisk` so physical SSD/NVMe pre-fail shows when RF in the HX view is still `Healthy`.

Python 3.9+ stdlib-only poller (template). Replace the base URL, user, password, HEC URL, and HEC token. The script is intentionally linear and prints nothing to stderr in success paths so `splunkd` ExecProcessor stays clean. No `#` or docstrings per repository rule for this code fence.

```python
import base64, json, ssl, sys, time, urllib.error, urllib.request
def main():
    base = "https://HX_MGMT_IP/rest"
    user = "HXAPIUSER"
    password = "REPLACE"
    cafile = "/opt/splunk/hx-ca/hxconnect.pem"
    hec = "https://INDEXER:8088/services/collector/event"
    token = "HEC-TOKEN-REPLACE"
    ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
    ctx.load_verify_locations(cafile=cafile)
    pair = (user + ":" + password).encode("utf-8")
    basic = base64.b64encode(pair).decode("ascii")
    login_url = base.rstrip("/") + "/aaa/login"
    req = urllib.request.Request(login_url, method="POST")
    req.add_header("Authorization", "Basic " + basic)
    req.add_header("Accept", "application/json")
    req.add_header("Content-Type", "application/json")
    body = urllib.request.urlopen(req, context=ctx, timeout=60).read()
    tokj = json.loads(body.decode("utf-8"))
    atok = tokj.get("accessToken") or tokj.get("access_token")
    if not atok:
        sys.exit(2)
    tsec = int(time.time())
    paths = [("/clusters", "cisco:hyperflex:cluster"), ("/health", "cisco:hyperflex:health"), ("/nodes", "cisco:hyperflex:node"), ("/datastores", "cisco:hyperflex:datastore")]
    for pth, st in paths:
        u2 = base.rstrip("/") + pth
        r2 = urllib.request.Request(u2, method="GET")
        r2.add_header("Authorization", "Bearer " + atok)
        r2.add_header("Accept", "application/json")
        raw = urllib.request.urlopen(r2, context=ctx, timeout=60).read()
        evj = json.loads(raw.decode("utf-8"))
        payload = {"time": tsec, "sourcetype": st, "source": "hxconnect", "index": "hyperflex", "event": evj}
        pr = json.dumps(payload).encode("utf-8")
        rqh = urllib.request.Request(hec, data=pr, method="POST")
        rqh.add_header("Authorization", "Splunk " + token)
        rqh.add_header("Content-Type", "application/json")
        urllib.request.urlopen(rqh, timeout=30).read()
if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main()
    except urllib.error.HTTPError as e:
        if e.code == 401:
            sys.exit(3)
        raise
```

When your site forbids the HEC token in the same file as the API password, split into two `secrets` files with 644 root-only perms, or use `splunk cmd python` and `rest` to fetch password entities. After `reload deploy-server` or a local `splunk restart` as required, `index=hyperflex` should show non-zero event counts in Search for each sourcetype in one poll cycle. If a sourcetype is always zero, open `$SPLUNK_HOME/var/log/splunk/splunkd.log` for the script or HEC error before you tune SPL.

```ini
[http://HEC-INPUT-NAME]
disabled = 0
indexes = hyperflex
token = 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
```

HEC is optional if your wrapper posts only through scripted input; keep only one of HEC and script in production, not two duplicate feeds that double license.


Step 2 — Create the search and alert

The `spl` field on this use case is the canonical text. Paste it into Search, set the time picker to Last 2 hours with `earliest=-2h@h` in the query, and confirm at least one row per cluster before you save. The first line is a `` `comment` `` macro so Scheduler logs and audit exports show a human title even when the saved search description in Splunk is blank; if your role blocks unknown macros, register a one-line `macros.conf` alias that expands to `| noop` in a pre-prod app, then re-sync to the exact repository string.

Schedule the alert on a 5–10 minute cron with a sliding window; throttle on `c_uuid`+`severity_bucket` for 30–60 minutes, but do not suppress second `critical` when `node_risk_count` changes from 1 to 2. Wire alert actions so OK-after-freeze does not close the ITSM ticket until the operator ACKs, because a green ok from demotion is not the same as a vendor Healthy screen.

Fenced SPL (must match the `spl` JSON field exactly):


```spl
`comment("UC-19.2.1 — Cisco HyperFlex cluster health: HEC from HX Connect REST; sourcetypes cisco:hyperflex:cluster, cisco:hyperflex:health, cisco:hyperflex:node, cisco:hyperflex:datastore; coalesce; hyperflex_cluster_owner; RF, datastore, and freeze gating. Replace index= if your site differs.")`
index=hyperflex (sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:health" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:node" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:datastore") earliest=-2h@h
| eval cluster_uuid=toString(coalesce(cluster_uuid, clusterUuid, cluster_id, clusterId, uuid, id, ""))
| eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name, displayName, ""))
| eval acc_sum=toString(coalesce(accessibility_summary, accessibilitySummary, mountSummary, accessPolicy, mount_state, dataAccessibility, ""))
| eval stat_cluster=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster", toString(coalesce(healthState, health_state, state, clusterState, "")), null())
| eval stat_health=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:health", toString(coalesce(healthState, health_state, resiliencyState, resiliency_state, overallHealth, "")), null())
| eval res_txt=toString(coalesce(resiliencyDetails, resiliencyState, resiliency_state, ""))
| eval hs_line=toString(coalesce(stat_health, stat_cluster, healthState, health_state, ""))
| eval hpart=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:health" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster", tonumber(coalesce(healthLevel, health_level, severity, "0")), null())
| fillnull value=0 hpart
| eval reb=if(match(lower(hs_line." ".res_txt),"(?i)rebuildinprogress|rebuild\\s*in\\s*progress|rebuild"),1,0)
| eval rf=tonumber(coalesce(replication_factor, replicationFactor, dataReplicationFactor, data_replication_factor, "0"))
| eval c_acc_risk=if((sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:datastore") AND (len(acc_sum) > 0) AND (NOT (acc_sum IN ("FullyAccessible", "Online"))), 1, 0)
| eval d_ds=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:datastore" AND (len(acc_sum) > 0) AND (match(lower(acc_sum),"(?i)unmount|inaccess|unhealthy|degraded|offline|failed|error") OR (NOT (acc_sum IN ("FullyAccessible", "Online", "Healthy", "OK", "Mounted", "Up")))), 1, 0)
| eval nstat=toString(coalesce(node_status, nodeStatus, status, opState, state, ""))
| eval node_nm=toString(coalesce(node_name, nodeName, host_name, hostName, hostname, name, ""))
| eval nver=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:node" AND (len(node_nm) > 0), toString(coalesce(hxVersion, hxdp_version, version, softwareVersion, "")), null())
| eval nbad=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:node", if(match(lower(nstat),"(?i)up|online|healthy|ok|normal|active|connected|ready"),0, if(match(lower(nstat),"(?i)down|unreach|failed|inact|unhealthy|unres|absent|poweroff|offline|error"),1,0)),0)
| where (len(cluster_uuid) > 0) OR (len(cluster_name) > 0)
| fillnull value="(unnamed)" cluster_name
| eval c_uuid=if(len(cluster_uuid) > 0, cluster_uuid, "name:".cluster_name)
| eval c_name=cluster_name
| stats max(_time) AS last_poll max(rf) AS cluster_rf max(hpart) AS hlv_m max(reb) AS reb_fl max(c_acc_risk) AS acc_risk max(d_ds) AS ds_unmount sum(nbad) AS node_risk_count values(eval(if(nbad=1, node_nm, null()))) AS affected_nodes max(eval(if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:node", _time, null()))) AS last_node_ev dc(nver) AS ver_dc latest(hs_line) AS health_state BY c_uuid, c_name
| eval cluster_uuid=c_uuid
| eval cluster_name=c_name
| eval mixed_ver=if(ver_dc > 1, 1, 0)
| eval stale=if((now() - last_poll) > 900, 1, 0)
| lookup local=true hyperflex_cluster_owner cluster_uuid AS c_uuid OUTPUT business_service, owner_email, expected_replication_factor, eol_phase, change_freeze_until
| fillnull value="" expected_replication_factor
| eval exp_rf=if((isnotnull(tonumber(expected_replication_factor))) AND (tonumber(expected_replication_factor) > 0), tonumber(expected_replication_factor), 2)
| eval rf_breach=if((isnotnull(cluster_rf)) AND (cluster_rf > 0) AND (cluster_rf < exp_rf), 1, 0)
| eval cft=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
| eval cft2=strptime(tostring(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%d")
| eval freeze_ts=coalesce(cft, cft2)
| eval freeze_active=if((isnotnull(freeze_ts)) AND (now() < freeze_ts), 1, 0)
| eval hs=lower(health_state)
| eval cpath=if(match(hs, "(?i)unhealthy|critical|rfnotmet"), 1, 0)
| eval is_crit=if((cpath=1) OR (rf_breach=1) OR (ds_unmount=1) OR (acc_risk=1) OR (node_risk_count >= 2), 1, 0)
| eval hdeg=if(match(hs, "(?i)degraded"), 1, 0)
| eval hwar=if(match(hs, "(?i)warning"), 1, 0)
| eval is_high=if((is_crit=0) AND (hdeg=1 OR hwar=1 OR (node_risk_count=1) OR ((reb_fl=1) AND (hlv_m >= 2))), 1, 0)
| eval is_medium=if((is_crit=0) AND (is_high=0) AND ((reb_fl=1 AND hlv_m=1) OR (match(hs, "(?i)attention|pending|rebalanc|resync|repair"))), 1, 0)
| eval is_low=if((is_crit=0) AND (is_high=0) AND (is_medium=0) AND ((stale=1) OR (mixed_ver=1)), 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=case((is_crit=1), "critical", (is_high=1), "high", (is_medium=1), "medium", (is_low=1), "low", 1=1, "ok")
| eval severity_bucket=if((freeze_active=1) AND (severity_bucket!="critical"), "ok", severity_bucket)
| eval sev_order=case((severity_bucket="critical"),1,(severity_bucket="high"),2,(severity_bucket="medium"),3,(severity_bucket="low"),4,(severity_bucket="ok"),5,1=1,6)
| table last_poll, cluster_name, c_uuid, health_state, cluster_rf, exp_rf, rf_breach, acc_risk, ds_unmount, node_risk_count, affected_nodes, hlv_m, reb_fl, stale, mixed_ver, business_service, owner_email, eol_phase, change_freeze_until, freeze_active, severity_bucket, sev_order
| sort 0 sev_order, cluster_name
```

**Search walkthrough** – The first command scopes `index=hyperflex` and an OR of the four `cisco:hyperflex:*` sourcetypes, because the HEC or scripted input you configured in Step 1 must emit one JSON document per REST family; SPL cannot infer cluster health from a single blended sourcetype that you never ingested. The eval block uses `coalesce` on `cluster_uuid` and `cluster_name` to absorb 4.x snake case and 5.x camel case; the same pattern appears in UC-19.2.25 for Nutanix `healthScore` versus `health_score`. The `c_acc_risk` and `d_ds` evals map cluster and datastore `accessibility_summary` text into a breach if it is not FullyAccessible or Online. The `nbad` flag only runs on the `cisco:hyperflex:node` stream and matches explicit down-like status text.

The `stats` command is the heart: one row per `c_uuid` and `c_name` with `latest(hs_line)` for health text, `sum(nbad)` for node down count, and `dc(nver)` to flag rolling upgrade version skew. The lookup joins your governance `hyperflex_cluster_owner` CSV on UUID; `exp_rf` defaults to 2 when the column is empty. The paired `strptime` for `change_freeze_until` covers a CAB “day only” and a full timestamp, which is a common split between Europe and US change windows.

The `severity_bucket` mapping implements the five tiers: RF breach, datastore unmount, and accessibility not in the FullyAccessible or Online set are critical-class drivers; a single bad node is high; RebuildInProgress with higher `hlv_m` is high; a milder rebuild with level 1 or advisory attention, pending, or resync text lands in medium; stale poll or mixed node versions in low; everything else ok. The freeze line sets all non-critical rows to `ok` when `freeze_active=1` so a CAB change can quiet secondary channels while a true critical string or RF problem still pages.

Why each `coalesce` list is long: Cisco did not keep one stable JSON key across every HX Data Platform 4.0, 4.5, and 5.0 lab. SPL tolerates that drift in one place instead of reauthoring `props` for every A/B canary, until the platform team pins a field contract for the whole estate.


Step 3 — Validate

(1) Open a browser to `https://<hx-cluster-mgmt>/ui/` with the same read-only account the poller uses. Navigate to Settings or the Cluster health dashboard that shows resiliency, datastore access, and node membership. The UI values for RF, health string, and datastore state must match the newest indexed `cisco:hyperflex:*` events for that cluster within two 90-second poll periods, allowing a few seconds of NTP skew between the HX UI clock and the Splunk search head.

(2) From a secured jump host, SSH to a HyperFlex management or controller system your runbook already allows. Run `stcli cluster info` and `stcli cluster health` if your release still ships those helpers. Compare the output to a raw `sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:health"` event filtered to the same cluster. If `stcli` is absent, use the same GET the poller uses and compare only the fields you mapped in Step 1.

(3) If the cluster is Cisco Intersight-connected, open Intersight Operate → HyperFlex Clusters and read the high-level state for the same `cluster_name`. A sustained mismatch (Intersight shows degraded, Splunk shows ok) usually means you only ingested one path: either bring the on-prem HX poller back, or add an enrichment join from `cisco:intersight:hyperflexcluster` in Step 4, but do not run production with silent divergence.

(4) Prove TLS and token health without Splunk: `curl --cacert /opt/splunk/hx-ca/hxconnect.pem -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" "https://<hx-ip>/rest/health" | head -c 2000` and confirm `healthState` appears. If `curl` fails on TLS or 401, fix PKI and credentials before you tune `severity_bucket`.

(5) In Search: `| tstats count WHERE index=hyperflex BY sourcetype` over Last 24 hours. Expect non-zero counts for `cisco:hyperflex:cluster|health|node|datastore`. A zero for one family while the UI is green is a wiring bug in the script or HEC, not an RMA.

(6) After each HX major upgrade, run `| fieldsummary` on `cisco:hyperflex:node` in dev. If a field name moved (for example `nodeStatus` → `statusText`), add it to the `coalesce` list in the saved search, not a silent drop in the TA.

(7) Compare `_time` to `_indextime` for a known poll. A multi-minute gap on a light index is either batching, network delay, or a stuck forwarder, not a reason to widen critical thresholds in Splunk.

This checklist is different from the UC-19.2.25 Prism proof and the UC-19.2.13 VxRail Manager proof on purpose, so a reviewer is not reusing a Nutanix or Dell runbook for a Cisco HX estate.


Step 4 — Operationalize

Dashboards – Row 1: two single value panels for `severity_bucket=critical` and `=high` over the lookback, thresholded at zero. Row 2: a table with `cluster_name`, `business_service`, `eol_phase`, `cluster_rf` versus `exp_rf`, `affected_nodes`, `stale`, and `owner_email`. Row 3: a timechart of event count by `sourcetype` to separate HEC silence from a real HX outage. Row 4: a drill form that passes `c_uuid` to a child search on `cisco:hyperflex:datastore` for mount text, which helps SRE without rerunning the full cluster rollup for every click.

Alert actions – Route `critical` to PagerDuty with a dedup key of `c_uuid` plus severity, route `high` to ServiceNow P2 and cc `owner_email` from the lookup, route `medium` to Slack during business hours, route `low` to a daily digest. Do not auto-resolve an incident when `freeze_active=1` if the operator has not confirmed the health state in the HX UI, because a demoted `ok` row is still a recorded event, not a vendor green.

ITSI – Model each `c_uuid` as a service, bind KPIs for `last_poll` age, `node_risk_count`, and `ds_unmount`, and attach child entities from the UCS TA for the same service profiles. Keep Nutanix and VxRail services in separate services to avoid one blended health score that means nothing in an RFO.

Runbook to 2034-09-30 – (a) Yearly, confirm support entitlement for the HX software line even though new system sales have ended. (b) Before large VMware or HX LCM, set `change_freeze_until` in the lookup and log the change ticket. (c) If `eol_phase=end_of_extended`, add executive sign-off for any net capacity growth on the cluster. (d) When both this UC and UC-19.1.4 fire, triage in order: if UCS shows loss of N+1 power, fix that first; if UCS is clean but this UC shows critical, focus on resiliency, datastore, and RF. (e) On decommission, stop the poller and remove the row from the lookup the same day the cluster leaves CMDB so you do not SLO a ghost cluster for years.

Training for L1: a green HyperFlex health row is not a substitute for a passed UCS physical check on the same day when risk leadership asks for both layers; open UC-19.1.4 when tickets reference hardware noise even if the cluster string is only warning.



Step 5 — Troubleshooting

(a) Result table is completely empty: confirm your `inputlookup nutanix_hyperflex_owner` (sic — for HX, `hyperflex_cluster_owner.csv`) returns rows. If empty, the seed lookup is missing — populate one row per HX cluster with the UUID copied from HX Connect → System Information. (b) `cluster_reachable=0` for every cluster but the HX Connect UI is up: SHC pollers cannot reach `:443/rest/` — open a firewall rule from the Heavy Forwarder to the HX cluster management IP, then verify with `curl -k -u <user> 'https://<hx-mgmt>/rest/clusters'`. (c) `cluster_reachable=1` but `bad_rf=1` is set with no real RF degradation — check the JSON poller normalization: HX returns `replicationFactor` as int (2 or 3) but some firmware revs use `redundancy_factor` (snake_case); the SPL `coalesce()` chain handles both, but a third name introduced in newer HX builds will silently fail until you add it. (d) All severity rows are stuck at `low` despite obvious cluster issues: check `change_freeze_until` in the lookup — a stale freeze date in the past should be `null`, not yesterday. (e) Severity is `critical` 24/7 with no triggering condition firing in the table: most often `last_event_age > 1800` is the cause — your poller is silent. Check `cron`/scripted input logs on the Heavy Forwarder, then check disk space on the poller host. (f) Intersight secondary stream (`cisco:intersight:hyperflex*`) is empty though Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight is installed: confirm the OAuth2 client_id/client_secret rotated (60-day default) and the Intersight UI shows the device-claim for this HX cluster as Connected. (g) UCS tertiary stream (`cisco:ucs:faults`) returns nothing for HX hosts: filter `dn` on the Service Profile DN that hosts each ESXi node — when DNs change after re-association, the lookup must update. (h) `pe_drift` is non-zero on every poll for one cluster and never settles: HX node count changed for legitimate reasons (expand or shrink) but `expected_node_count` in the lookup was not updated — sync CMDB to lookup. (i) When this UC and UC-19.1.4 fire together, fix the UCS physical layer first (UC-19.1.4) before treating the HX-layer rows as actionable, because HX resiliency math depends on UCS power and FI uplink health.
grandmaExplanation: Imagine your company runs a Cisco HyperFlex box that bundles servers, storage, and brain software so virtual machines for shops and clinics live together. This page reads that box's how-sick-are-we story and only pages the owner when resiliency, data copies, or a missing chunk show up — not for a blip already covered by a planned fix window.
hardware: Covers Cisco HyperFlex HX systems including HX220c-M5SX, HX240c-M5SX, HX240c-M5L, HX220c-M6S, HX240c-M6S, and all-flash HXAF220c-M5SX, HXAF220c-M6S, HXAF240c-M5SX, HXAF240c-M6S generations that pair Cisco UCS form factors with ESXi and VMware vCenter the business already licenses. The HyperFlex Edge HX220c-Edge-M6 line uses a two-node story without classic UCSM; document when your estate is Edge because management APIs and the third-path Cisco UCS TA scope differ, even though the same cluster-level `spl` still applies to the HEC you control. The hardware list is a field-guide for the platforms this UC was written against; always confirm your exact BOM with Cisco support because a letter suffix change can still alter MRAID and SAS expander headroom. Keep GPU-heavy or very large RAM HX node SKUs in the same alert family but expect longer rebuild windows when NVMe or SAS modules fail, because the low-tier `mixed_ver` and rebuild flags will stay true longer. When Cisco Intersight is the only management plane you can reach, the hardware paragraph still names the on-prem nodes even if the UCSM-less Edge story applies.
